front: What is the formal definition of an exterior point of a set A in a metric space (X, d)?
back: A point x ∈ X is an exterior point of A if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is completely contained in the complement of A, i.e., B(x, ε) ⊆ A^c.

front: What is the definition of a boundary point of a set A in a metric space?
back: A point x ∈ X is a boundary point of A if every open ball centered at x intersects both A and its complement A^c. That is, for every ε > 0, B(x, ε) ∩ A ≠ ∅ and B(x, ε) ∩ A^c ≠ ∅.

front: How are the set of exterior points, interior points, and boundary points of a set A related?
back: These three sets are mutually disjoint and their union is the whole metric space X. The exterior of A is the interior of the complement A^c, and the boundary of A is the same as the boundary of A^c.

front: What is the notation for the set of all exterior points of a set A?
back: The set of all exterior points of A is denoted by ext(A).

front: True or False: A point can be simultaneously an interior point and a boundary point of a set.
back: False. By definition, an interior point has a neighborhood entirely within A, while a boundary point has every neighborhood intersecting the complement A^c. These are mutually exclusive properties.
